title: The Pain in My Eye, or the Strength of the Kazhagam: Which Should Command My Attention?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil reading copy corrects four OCR errors against the source scans: `முலலை` to `முல்லை`, `தொடர்ப் பிரச்சாரம்` to `தொடர் பிரச்சாரம்`, `1911` to `1971`, and `அகாவால்` to `அகர்வால்`. The boxed passage on source page 207 is retained even though the same argument recurs later, because that repetition is present in the printed source. The canonical Tamil source file is unchanged.
paragraphs: In the letter I write to you today, I wish to recount the car accident I met with in 1953, how one of my eyes was damaged in it, and how that injury has continued to trouble me to this day.

In 1953, a public meeting was arranged in my honour at Paramakudi in Mugavai district. I left Chennai by car at three in the afternoon to attend it. We had to reach Paramakudi at the appointed time, so the car was travelling rather fast. As we neared Tiruchi, a long-horned ram charged into the car's radiator. The car broke down, and I hired another car and set out for Paramakudi. Anbil Dharmalingam, Tiruchi Parangusam and Tiruvarur Thennan travelled with me. Madurai Muthu kept the meeting going until I reached the stage. I began speaking as soon as I arrived, and the meeting concluded at one in the morning. A special meeting was scheduled for the following day at the Devar Hall in Tiruchi. On the return journey, weariness overcame my friends and me, and we dozed off. The comrade driving the hired car also nodded off for a moment. Near the travellers' bungalow at Tiruppathur on the road to Tiruchi, the car struck a milestone, shattered it, and then crashed into the front gate of the bungalow before coming to a halt. We were tossed about inside the car. None of my friends was injured, but blood gushed from my nose.
After receiving first aid, we reached Tiruchi. By the following morning my whole face had swollen, and pain had begun in my left eye.

Despite the pain, I attended the events in Tiruchi and Kanyakumari district and returned to Chennai. My friend Mullai Sakthi then insisted on taking me to the hospital in Vellore. The doctor there said that a tiny nerve inside the eye had been torn and that, for at least six months, I must neither write nor speak at meetings nor read. Could someone engaged in public life like me possibly observe all those restrictions?

You know very well that I have continued to perform my duty without regard for the pain in my eye because the Kazhagam must grow in strength and splendour. It is not enough merely to understand that. Several people may have submitted applications seeking the nomination in a single constituency, but only one person can be fielded there on behalf of our Kazhagam. Some constituencies may also have to be allotted to our allied parties. Headquarters has announced that applicants for those constituencies will be refunded the amount they paid to the Kazhagam headquarters. I ask you to consider all this carefully and work with the single aim of securing victory for the Kazhagam.

To fulfil the wishes of my close friends in politics and the world of art, the ideal actor S. S. Rajendran and Nadigamani T. V. Narayanasamy, I was writing the final scenes of the play *Manimakudam* in a Chennai hotel when a sudden agony struck my left eye as though a spear had pierced it! I flung away the pen and paper in my hands and cried out, “Ayyo!”

Somehow I reached home. My family broke down on seeing me. My left eye had swollen enormously. The stabbing pain tore through my very life. It was unbearable. Dr Muthiah, one of Chennai's finest physicians, examined me and said I must be admitted to hospital immediately. I was admitted to the eye hospital and underwent surgery. He operated on me twelve times.

From 1953–54 onward, I continued my political journey while enduring the suffering caused by that damaged eye. In September 1967, I was caught in another terrible car accident, and the pain already present in the eye grew still worse. Doctors Muthiah, Abraham, Ramalingam and Madurai Venkatasamy treated me from time to time, yet the needle-like stabbing pain in the eye persisted. Indeed, it continues to torment me even today.

Meanwhile, through the extraordinary united labour and continuous campaigning of everyone in the 1967 general election, the DMK won and formed the government under Anna's leadership. But after Anna left us all in 1969, the pain in my eye continued even when I assumed office as Chief Minister.

In 1971, acting on the advice of India's finest eye specialists, I decided to travel abroad for eye surgery and went to the United States. At Johns Hopkins Hospital in Baltimore, the renowned specialist Dr Maumenee operated on me. Although the surgery somewhat reduced the eye pain that had continually troubled me, it still returned from time to time. Indeed, it continues to torment me to this very day. Which is greater: the pain in that eye, or the pain inflicted on my mind from time to time by those who once travelled with me on my political journey? Through such questions, my work for the Kazhagam and my public service have continued without pause. Even now, whenever the eye pain becomes severe, I receive treatment from Dr Agarwal and, after his passing, from his son Amar Agarwal, and continue my political work.
However much pain and distress I may face, you must understand that I continue to work because of the feeling that rises within me that I must still serve our Kazhagam and the people of Tamil Nadu.

Meanwhile, preparations for the 2016 general election have begun, and Udanpirappugal of the Kazhagam are now submitting applications seeking party nominations. Every day, I receive information from headquarters on how many people have collected applications and how many have submitted them. You will have seen in *Murasoli*, published alongside their names, that no fewer than about fifty people come to me every morning and evening, family after family, and contribute one thousand or two thousand rupees to the election fund! The fund collected in this way has now reached Rs 26 crore, 84 lakh, 30 thousand and 936. That makes me forget the pain in my eye. You know very well that I continue to perform my duty without regard for that pain because the Kazhagam must grow in strength and splendour. It is not enough merely to understand that. Several people may have submitted applications seeking the nomination in a single constituency, but only one person can be fielded there on behalf of our Kazhagam.

Some constituencies may also have to be allotted to our allied parties. Headquarters has announced that applicants for those constituencies will be refunded the amount they paid to the Kazhagam headquarters. I ask you to consider all this carefully and work with the single aim of securing victory for the Kazhagam. Understand that I am working with the question before me: the pain in my eye, or the strength of the Kazhagam—which should command my attention? With the confidence that, as always, you too will labour tirelessly for the Kazhagam's victory, and with my appeal that you must do so, I bring this letter to a close.
